title: Subscription Trial Validation: Sequential Low-Amount Authorization Testing via Free Trial Mechanisms
narrative: Fraudsters exploit subscription services offering free trials or $1 authorization holds to validate stolen card credentials without triggering fraud alerts from significant charges. A single device or IP address initiates 8-15 separate subscription trials within 45-90 minutes, each generating a $0.00-$1.00 authorization on different card numbers. The attacker uses ProductCD 'W' (digital subscription) transactions with identical P_emaildomain patterns (often temporary email services like 'guerrillamail.com' or 'tempmail.org') but rotating card1 values. Each authorization attempt includes matching billing/shipping addresses (dist1 = 0) to avoid AVS mismatches. Successful authorizations indicate valid cards for later high-value fraud, while declined attempts are immediately discarded. The pattern shows card1_txn_count_1day = 1 for each card but device_txn_count_1day exceeding 10, with TransactionAmt consistently under $1.50 and all transactions occurring during is_night = 1 periods (txn_hour between 1-5 AM) to evade real-time monitoring.
indicators: 8-15 transactions from single device_id or IP within 45-90 minute window
TransactionAmt values uniformly $0.00-$1.50 across all attempts with ProductCD = 'W'
P_emaildomain from temporary email services (guerrillamail, tempmail, 10minutemail) with sequential numeric prefixes
card1_txn_count_1day = 1 for each unique card1 while device_txn_count_1day > 10
dist1 = 0 (perfect address match) across all transactions despite rotating card credentials
All transactions concentrated in txn_hour 1-5 (is_night = 1) avoiding business hours
card1_amt_zscore near 0 for each individual card but device_amt_zscore > 3.5
summary: A single device creates 8-15 separate subscription trials in under 90 minutes, each with a different card number but identical email domains from temporary services and perfect address matches, all for amounts under $1.50 during overnight hours (1-5 AM).
reasoning: Criminals test stolen card credentials through low-value authorization holds that won't alert cardholders or trigger fraud systems, discarding declined cards immediately while keeping validated ones for future high-value purchases.
indicator_explanations: Same device or IP address fires off a rapid sequence of attempts in under two hours
All transaction amounts cluster in the penny-to-dollar range for trial subscriptions
Email addresses rotate through disposable services with sequential numbered prefixes
Each card appears for the first time while the device racks up many attempts
Billing address stays identical despite constantly rotating through different cards
Attack happens in the middle of the night when monitoring is weakest
Each card shows normal amounts individually but the device pattern is abnormally high
